title: JavaScript Hoisting Tricky Questions
description: Test your understanding of tricky hoisting behavior with var, let, const, temporal dead zone, function declarations, and function expressions.
author: DailyCoder

questions.explanation: `var` declarations are hoisted and initialized to `undefined`. The assignment to `10` happens later.
`let` is hoisted but not initialized. Accessing it before the declaration line is a temporal dead zone error.

options.text: It throws a `TypeError` because strings cannot be constants

questions.explanation: `const` is also in the temporal dead zone until its declaration line runs.
Function declarations are fully hoisted, so the function can be called before its declaration appears.
`var sayHi` is hoisted as `undefined`, but the function assignment happens later. Calling `undefined` causes a `TypeError`.
The function expression is assigned to a `const`, so `sayHi` cannot be accessed before initialization.
The local `var count` is hoisted inside `showCount` and shadows the global `count`, but its assignment to `2` has not happened yet.
The block-scoped `status` shadows the global `status`, but it is in the temporal dead zone until its declaration line.
`var message` is hoisted to the function scope even though the `if` body does not run. The assignment never happens, so the value is `undefined`.
`let message` is block-scoped to the `if` block. It does not exist outside that block.

options.text: JavaScript physically moves declarations to the top of your file
Hoisting describes how JavaScript prepares declarations before executing code

questions.explanation: Hoisting is a mental model for JavaScript's setup phase. The engine prepares declarations before execution; it does not physically rewrite your code.
`typeof` on a truly undeclared variable returns `undefined`, but `declaredLater` exists in the TDZ, so `typeof declaredLater` throws a `ReferenceError`.
`var` is not block-scoped, so `i` remains available after the loop. The loop stops when `i` becomes `2`.
`let i` is scoped to the loop, so it is not available outside the loop.
`inner` is hoisted within `outer`, so the first call works. It is not available outside `outer`, so the second call throws a `ReferenceError`.
